_note: Per-problem detail keyed by slug. aiSummary = our OWN re-written problem statement (never LeetCode's wording — algorithmic concepts aren't copyrightable, specific phrasing is). starterCode = a runnable Python blueprint (function signature + a sample call so it traces in the Playground). Optional viz = problem-specific visualizer config: { key, input } where key matches lib/patterns.js and input is passed to that visualizer (TwoPointerViz + BinarySearchViz are input-aware today). aiSummary fields are original drafts written for this app; replace/refine with your own ChatGPT/Gemini rewrites anytime — the UI reads this file as-is. Set draft:false once reviewed.

two-sum.goal: Given a list of integers `nums` and a target integer `target`, return the indices of the two distinct numbers that add up to `target`. Exactly one valid pair exists, each element may be used once, and the order of the two indices doesn't matter.
two-sum.constraints: 2 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10,000 · -10^9 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9 · -10^9 ≤ target ≤ 10^9 · exactly one solution exists.
two-sum.aiSummary:
Given a list of integers `nums` and a target integer `target`, find the two distinct positions whose values add up to `target`, and return them as a pair of indices. Exactly one valid pair is guaranteed to exist, and you may use each element at most once. The order of the two indices in your answer doesn't matter.

Example: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9 → [0, 1], because nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9.

Constraints: 2 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10,000 · -10^9 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9 · -10^9 ≤ target ≤ 10^9 · exactly one solution exists.

# Sample run (edit the input to experiment)

steps.title: Say the goal in your own words
steps.prompt: Before any code: what exactly must two_sum return? Write it as a # comment above the function — say what the two numbers must do, and what you return instead of the numbers themselves.
hints: Not the values — the answer is WHERE they live in the list.
Two indices i and j (any order) such that nums[i] + nums[j] == target.

steps.title: Write the obvious version first
steps.prompt: Try every pair: an outer loop over each index, an inner loop over the LATER indices, return the pair that adds up to target. Make the sample print [0, 1], then press OK.
hints: Two nested loops: for i in range(len(nums)), and inside it for j in range(i + 1, len(nums)).
Inside the inner loop: if nums[i] + nums[j] == target: return [i, j]

steps.title: Now look at what it costs
steps.prompt: It works — but look at the complexity check under the editor: nested loops mean ~N² pair-checks. With 10,000 numbers that is ~50 million. Say (to yourself) WHICH loop you would delete if you could, then press OK.
hints: The inner loop only exists to answer one question: "have I seen the number that completes this one?"

steps.title: Trade space for time
steps.prompt: The question "have I seen target - x?" can be O(1) if you REMEMBER numbers as you pass them. Delete the inner loop and create an empty dict named seen before the (single) remaining loop — it will map each value to its index.
hints: seen = {} on the line before the loop.
Keep one loop over indices and values: for i, x in enumerate(nums):

steps.title: Ask first, remember after
steps.prompt: Inside the loop, for each value x: FIRST ask whether target - x is already in seen (if it is, you have both indices — return them), and only THEN store x in seen. Make the sample print [0, 1] again.
hints: Membership test: if target - x in seen: return [seen[target - x], i]
After the if (not before it): seen[x] = i

steps.title: The duplicate trap
steps.prompt: Why "ask first, remember after"? Consider nums = [3, 3], target = 6 — the answer needs BOTH threes. If you stored x before asking, the dict would answer with the number itself. Run your solution against this case in the sample line, then press OK.
hints: If this fails with [0, 0], you are inserting into seen before the membership test — swap the order.

valid-palindrome.goal: Decide whether a string reads the same forwards and backwards after ignoring case and keeping only alphanumeric characters. Return True or False; an empty filtered string counts as a palindrome.
valid-palindrome.constraints: 1 ≤ len(s) ≤ 200,000 · s consists of printable ASCII characters.
valid-palindrome.examples: "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama" → True (it reduces to "amanaplanacanalpanama"). "race a car" → False.

Examples: "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama" → True (it reduces to "amanaplanacanalpanama"). "race a car" → False.

Constraints: 1 ≤ len(s) ≤ 200,000 · s consists of printable ASCII characters.

    pass


# Sample run (edit the input to experiment)
print(is_palindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"))  # expected: True

input.string: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama

steps.title: Say the rule you are checking
steps.prompt: In a # comment: what makes s a palindrome HERE? Name the two things you must ignore before comparing anything (look closely at the sample input).
hints: The sample has spaces, punctuation, and mixed case — which of those survive into the comparison?
Ignore non-alphanumeric characters entirely; compare case-insensitively.

steps.title: Clean the string first
steps.prompt: Build a cleaned version of s: keep only alphanumeric characters, all lowercased. Store it (e.g. t) — do not compare anything yet.
hints: c.isalnum() tests one character; c.lower() lowercases it.
One line does it: t = [c.lower() for c in s if c.isalnum()]

steps.title: Pick your comparison
steps.prompt: Two clean ways to finish: compare t to its reverse, or walk two pointers inward from both ends. Decide which you will write — the reverse-compare is one line; two pointers is the interview-classic O(1)-extra-space version.
hints: t[::-1] reverses a list or string.
Two pointers: i at 0, j at the end, walk while i < j.

steps.title: Make it answer
steps.prompt: Finish is_palindrome with your chosen strategy and make the sample print True.
hints: Reverse-compare: return t == t[::-1]
Two pointers: while i < j: if t[i] != t[j]: return False; move both inward; return True after the loop.

tests.call: is_palindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama")

steps.title: The empty-after-cleaning trap
steps.prompt: Two classics: a string that is ALL punctuation (cleans to empty — a palindrome by definition) and "0P" (digit + letter: cleaned "0p" is NOT a palindrome). Predict both answers, then press OK to test.
hints: An empty sequence equals its own reverse — it should return True.
Digits must survive your filter: isalnum() keeps them, isalpha() silently drops them and breaks "0P".

tests.call: is_palindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama")

reverse-linked-list.aiSummary:
Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse it so the last node becomes the new head, and return that new head.

Example: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 becomes 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1.

Constraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 5,000 · -5,000 ≤ node value ≤ 5,000 · an iterative and a recursive solution both work.

steps.title: Reverse the arrows, not the values
steps.prompt: In a # comment: what actually changes when a linked list reverses? (Node values never move.) Say what each node’s .next must point at afterwards.
hints: Every node’s .next must point at the node that USED to be before it.
The old head becomes the tail (its .next becomes None); the old tail becomes the new head.

steps.title: Two travellers
steps.prompt: You will walk the list once, flipping arrows as you go. Before the loop set prev = None and cur = head, then write the loop that walks while cur is not None — do not flip anything yet.
hints: prev trails exactly one node behind cur the whole walk.
while cur: is the standard walk.

steps.title: Flip one arrow per step
steps.prompt: Inside the loop: save cur.next FIRST (or you lose the rest of the list), point cur.next at prev, then advance both travellers. After the loop, return the new head. Make the sample print [5, 4, 3, 2, 1].
hints: nxt = cur.next, then cur.next = prev, then prev = cur, then cur = nxt — the order matters.
When the loop ends, cur is None and prev stands on the old tail: return prev.
steps.reveal:
def reverse_list(head):
    prev = None
    cur = head
    while cur:
        nxt = cur.next
        cur.next = prev
        prev = cur
        cur = nxt
    return prev

steps.title: Empty and single
steps.prompt: Two inputs interviewers always probe: an empty list (head is None) and a single node. Convince yourself your loop handles both WITHOUT special cases, then press OK.
hints: With head None the loop never runs and you return prev, which is None — exactly right.

invert-binary-tree.aiSummary:
Given the root of a binary tree, swap every node's left and right child (mirror the whole tree) and return the root.

Example (level order): [4,2,7,1,3,6,9] becomes [4,7,2,9,6,3,1].

Constraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 100 · -100 ≤ node value ≤ 100.
invert-binary-tree.starterCode:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right


def invert_tree(root):
    # Mirror the tree: swap children at every node. Return root.
    # Your code here
    pass


def level_order(root):
    out, q = [], [root]
    while q:
        node = q.pop(0)
        if not node:
            continue
        out.append(node.val)
        q.append(node.left)
        q.append(node.right)
    return out


root = TreeNode(4, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(1), TreeNode(3)), TreeNode(7, TreeNode(6), TreeNode(9)))
print(level_order(invert_tree(root)))  # expected: [4, 7, 2, 9, 6, 3, 1]

plus-one-linked-list.starterCode:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

class Solution:
    def plusOne(self, head: ListNode) -> ListNode:
        # Your code here
        pass

# Helper to convert list to ListNode
def create_linked_list(arr):
    if not arr: return None
    head = ListNode(arr[0])
    current = head
    for i in range(1, len(arr)):
        current.next = ListNode(arr[i])
        current = current.next
    return head

# Helper to convert ListNode to list
def linked_list_to_list(head):
    arr = []
    current = head
    while current:
        arr.append(current.val)
        current = current.next
    return arr

head1 = create_linked_list([1,2,3])
result1 = Solution().plusOne(head1)
print(linked_list_to_list(result1)) # expected: [1,2,4]

head2 = create_linked_list([9,9,9])
result2 = Solution().plusOne(head2)
print(linked_list_to_list(result2)) # expected: [1,0,0,0]

guess-number-higher-or-lower-ii.goal: You are playing a number guessing game where you need to find a secret number between 1 and `n`. If your guess is wrong, you must pay an amount equal to your guess. The game ends when you guess correctly. Determine the minimum amount of money you must pay to guarantee a win, assuming optimal play.
guess-number-higher-or-lower-ii.examples:
Example 1:
Input: n = 10
Output: 16
Explanation: One optimal strategy to guarantee a win:
If the number is 1-10, you might guess 7. Cost: 7.
  If 7 is correct, you win. Total cost: 7.
  If 7 is too low (number is 8-10), you might guess 9. Cost: 7+9=16.
    If 9 is correct, you win. Total cost: 16.
    If 9 is too low (number is 10), you must guess 10. Cost: 7+9+10=26. (This path is not optimal)
    Let's reconsider: if 7 is too low (number is 8-10), you should guess 8. Cost: 7+8=15.
      If 8 is correct, you win. Total cost: 15.
      If 8 is too low (number is 9-10), you guess 9. Cost: 7+8+9=24.
        If 9 is correct, you win. Total cost: 24.
        If 9 is too low (number is 10), you guess 10. Cost: 7+8+9+10=34. (Still not optimal)

The optimal strategy for n=10 involves a more complex decision tree, leading to a guaranteed maximum cost of 16. For instance, if you start with 7, and it's too low (8-10), you then choose 9. If 9 is correct, total 7+9=16. If 9 is too low (10), you pay 7+9+10=26. This isn't the best. The optimal strategy is dynamic programming based.

longest-absolute-file-path.goal: Given a string representing a file system's directory and file structure, calculate the length of the longest absolute path to any file within that system.
longest-absolute-file-path.constraints: Input string represents a valid file system · File names contain '.' · 1 <= input.length <= 10^4.

find-the-difference.constraints: t.length == s.length + 1 · s and t consist of lowercase English letters.

elimination-game.goal: Simulate a game starting with numbers from 1 to `n`. In alternating rounds, remove every second number from left-to-right, then right-to-left, until only one number remains. Return this final number.

perfect-rectangle.goal: Given a collection of rectangles, each defined by its bottom-left and top-right coordinates, determine if these rectangles collectively form a single, perfect rectangle without any overlaps or gaps.
perfect-rectangle.constraints: 1 <= rectangles.length <= 2 * 10^4 · Each rectangle [x1, y1, x2, y2] has x1 < x2 and y1 < y2.
perfect-rectangle.examples:
rectangles = [[1,1,3,3],[3,1,4,2],[3,2,4,4],[1,3,2,4],[2,3,4,4]] -> True (These form a 3x3 square from (1,1) to (4,4)).
rectangles = [[1,1,2,3],[1,3,2,4],[3,1,4,2],[3,2,4,4]] -> False (There's a gap between the two sets of rectangles).

utf-8-validation.goal: Given an array of integers, where each integer represents a byte, determine if this sequence of bytes forms a valid UTF-8 encoding.
utf-8-validation.constraints: 1 <= data.length <= 10^4 · 0 <= data[i] <= 255 · Each integer represents 1 byte of data.
utf-8-validation.examples:
data = [197, 130, 1] -> True (197 (11000101) and 130 (10000010) form a valid 2-byte character, then 1 (00000001) is a valid 1-byte character).
data = [235, 140, 4] -> False (235 (11101011) expects two continuation bytes, but 140 (10001100) is one, and 4 (00000100) is not a continuation byte).

        self.left = left
        self.right = right

    def sumOfLeftLeaves(self, root: TreeNode) -> int:
        # Your code here
        pass

# Helper to build tree for example
def build_tree(nodes):
    if not nodes:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(nodes[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(nodes):
        current = queue.pop(0)
        if nodes[i] is not None:
            current.left = TreeNode(nodes[i])
            queue.append(current.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(nodes) and nodes[i] is not None:
            current.right = TreeNode(nodes[i])
            queue.append(current.right)
        i += 1
    return root

tree_root = build_tree([3,9,20,None,None,15,7])
print(Solution().sumOfLeftLeaves(tree_root))
# expected: 24

lfu-cache.goal: Design and implement an LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache. It should support get(key) and put(key, value) operations. When the cache reaches its capacity and a new item is inserted, it must evict the least frequently used item. If there's a tie in frequency, evict the least recently used item among those with the minimum frequency.
lfu-cache.constraints: capacity between 0 and 10^4 · key and value between 0 and 10^5 · At most 2 * 10^5 calls will be made to get and put.
lfu-cache.examples:
LFUCache cache = new LFUCache(2);
cache.put(1, 1); // Cache: {1:1} freq:1
cache.put(2, 2); // Cache: {1:1, 2:2} freq:1
cache.get(1);    // returns 1. Cache: {1:1} freq:2, {2:2} freq:1
cache.put(3, 3); // Evicts key 2 (LFU). Cache: {1:1, 3:3}. {1:1} freq:2, {3:3} freq:1
cache.get(2);    // returns -1 (not found)
Output of cache.get(1) is 1.

minimum-unique-word-abbreviation.goal: Given a target word and a list of other words, find the shortest possible abbreviation of the target word that is not an abbreviation of any word in the given list. If multiple shortest abbreviations exist, any one is acceptable.
minimum-unique-word-abbreviation.constraints: target and dictionary words consist of lowercase English letters · 1 <= target.length <= 20 · 0 <= dictionary.length <= 1000
minimum-unique-word-abbreviation.examples:
target = "apple", dictionary = ["blade"] -> "a4" (Other valid shortest abbreviations like "ap3" are also acceptable, as "a4" is not an abbreviation of "blade").
target = "apple", dictionary = ["plain", "amber"] -> "aple" ("a4" is an abbreviation of "amber", "ap3" is an abbreviation of "plain". "aple" is not an abbreviation of either.)

fizz-buzz.goal: Generate a list of strings representing numbers from 1 to `n`, with special rules: multiples of 3 become "Fizz", multiples of 5 become "Buzz", and multiples of both 3 and 5 become "FizzBuzz".

arithmetic-slices.goal: Count the total number of arithmetic slices present in a given integer array. An arithmetic slice is a contiguous subarray of at least three elements where the difference between any two consecutive elements is constant.

third-maximum-number.goal: Find the third largest distinct number in an array. If there are fewer than three distinct numbers, return the largest number in the array.
third-maximum-number.examples:
nums = [3, 2, 1] -> 1 (Distinct numbers are 3, 2, 1. The third maximum is 1).
nums = [1, 2] -> 2 (Only two distinct numbers, so return the maximum, which is 2).
nums = [2, 2, 3, 1] -> 1 (Distinct numbers are 3, 2, 1. The third maximum is 1. Duplicate '2's count as one distinct number).

add-strings.goal: Given two non-negative integers represented as strings, calculate their sum and return it as a string. You are not allowed to use any built-in BigInteger library or convert the strings directly to integers.
add-strings.constraints: 1 <= num1.length, num2.length <= 10^4 · num1 and num2 consist only of digits · num1 and num2 do not contain any leading zeros, except for the number "0" itself

partition-equal-subset-sum.goal: Determine if a given array of positive integers can be partitioned into two subsets such that the sum of elements in both subsets is equal.
partition-equal-subset-sum.examples:
nums = [1, 5, 11, 5] -> True (Can be partitioned into [1, 5, 5] and [11], both sum to 11).
nums = [1, 2, 3, 5] -> False (No way to partition into two equal sum subsets).

pacific-atlantic-water-flow.goal: Given a 2D grid representing elevations, find all cells from which water can flow to both the Pacific Ocean (top and left edges) and the Atlantic Ocean (bottom and right edges). Water flows from a cell to an adjacent cell if the adjacent cell has an elevation less than or equal to the current cell.
pacific-atlantic-water-flow.constraints: m == heights.length, n == heights[i].length · 1 <= m, n <= 200 · 0 <= heights[r][c] <= 10^5

sentence-screen-fitting.goal: Given a sentence (a list of words), the number of rows, and the number of columns of a screen, determine how many times the entire sentence can be typed on the screen. Words cannot be split, and each word must be separated by a single space.
sentence-screen-fitting.examples:
sentence = ["hello", "world"], rows = 2, cols = 8 -> 1
(Screen layout:
hello---
world---)
sentence = ["a", "b", "c"], rows = 1, cols = 10 -> 1
(Screen layout:
a b c a b )

battleships-in-a-board.goal: Count the number of battleships on a 2D board. Battleships are marked with 'X', and empty cells with '.'. Battleships can only be placed horizontally or vertically, and they cannot touch each other, even diagonally.
battleships-in-a-board.constraints: m == board.length, n == board[i].length · 1 <= m, n <= 200 · board[i][j] is 'X' or '.'

strong-password-checker.goal: Calculate the minimum number of changes (insertions, deletions, or replacements) needed to make a given password string 'strong'. A strong password must be between 6 and 20 characters long (inclusive), contain at least one lowercase letter, one uppercase letter, and one digit, and not have three or more repeating characters in a row.
strong-password-checker.examples:
s = "a" -> 5 (Needs 5 insertions to meet length and type requirements, e.g., "aA1bC").
s = "aA1" -> 3 (Needs 3 insertions to meet length requirement, e.g., "aA1bC").
s = "1337C0d3" -> 0 (Already a strong password).

maximum-xor-of-two-numbers-in-an-array.goal: Given an array of non-negative integers, find the maximum possible result of `nums[i] XOR nums[j]` where `i` and `j` are distinct indices.

valid-word-square.goal: Determine if a given sequence of words forms a valid word square. A sequence of words forms a valid word square if the k-th row and k-th column read the exact same string for all valid indices k.
valid-word-square.constraints: 1 <= words.length <= 500 · 1 <= words[i].length <= 500 · words[i] consists of lowercase English letters
valid-word-square.examples:
words = ["abcd", "bnrt", "crmy", "dtye"] -> True
(Row 0: "abcd", Col 0: "abcd"; Row 1: "bnrt", Col 1: "bnrt", etc.)
words = ["ball", "area", "lead", "lady"] -> False
(Row 1: "area", Col 1: "arel" - mismatch)

reconstruct-original-digits-from-english.goal: Given a non-empty string composed of shuffled English letters representing digits 0-9, reconstruct and return the original digits in ascending order.
reconstruct-original-digits-from-english.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^5 · s contains only lowercase English letters · It is guaranteed that s is a valid input and can be transformed into digits

longest-repeating-character-replacement.goal: Given a string `s` and an integer `k`, you can change any character in the string to any other uppercase English character at most `k` times. Find the length of the longest substring containing only repeating characters you can achieve.
longest-repeating-character-replacement.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^5 · s consists of only uppercase English letters · 0 <= k <= s.length
longest-repeating-character-replacement.examples:
s = "ABAB", k = 2 -> 4 (Replace the two 'A's with 'B's or two 'B's with 'A's to get "BBBB" or "AAAA").
s = "AABABBA", k = 1 -> 4 (Replace one 'A' in "AABBA" to get "AAABA" or "AABBB" to get "BBBBB". The longest possible is 4, e.g., "AABB" becomes "AAAA" by changing one 'B').

word-squares.goal: Given a list of words, find all possible word squares that can be formed. A word square is a sequence of words where the k-th row and k-th column read the exact same string, and all words in the square must be from the given list.
word-squares.constraints: 1 <= words.length <= 1000 · 1 <= words[i].length <= 5 · All words are unique and consist of lowercase English letters
word-squares.examples:
words = ["area","lead","wall","lady","ball"] -> [["ball","area","lead","lady"],["wall","area","lead","lady"]]
(Example squares:
ball   wall
area   area
lead   lead
lady   lady)

binary-tree-tilt.goal: Calculate the total 'tilt' of a binary tree. The tilt of a single node is the absolute difference between the sum of its left subtree's node values and the sum of its right subtree's node values. The total tree tilt is the sum of tilts of all nodes.
binary-tree-tilt.constraints: The number of nodes n is between 0 and 10^4 · Node values are between -1000 and 1000.
binary-tree-tilt.examples:
For `root = [4,2,9,null,null,null,7]` (representing 4 with left 2, right 9, and 9 with right 7):
    - Node 2 (leaf): left sum=0, right sum=0. Tilt = |0-0| = 0.
    - Node 7 (leaf): left sum=0, right sum=0. Tilt = |0-0| = 0.
    - Node 9 (child 7): left sum=0, right sum=7. Tilt = |0-7| = 7. (Node 9 value is 9, subtree sum 9+7=16)
    - Node 4 (children 2,9): left sum=2, right sum=16. Tilt = |2-16| = 14. (Node 4 value is 4, subtree sum 4+2+16=22)
    Total tilt = 0+0+7+14 = 21. Return `21`.
    (Note: The example `[4,2,9,3,5,None,7]` from LeetCode has more nodes. My example is simplified for clarity.)
    For `root = [4,2,9,3,5,null,7]` (as per LeetCode's example):
    - Node 3 (leaf): Tilt = 0. Subtree Sum = 3.
    - Node 5 (leaf): Tilt = 0. Subtree Sum = 5.
    - Node 7 (leaf): Tilt = 0. Subtree Sum = 7.
    - Node 2 (children 3,5): Left sum=3, Right sum=5. Tilt = |3-5|=2. Subtree Sum = 3+5+2=10.
    - Node 9 (child 7): Left sum=0, Right sum=7. Tilt = |0-7|=7. Subtree Sum = 9+7=16.
    - Node 4 (children 2,9): Left sum=10, Right sum=16. Tilt = |10-16|=6. Subtree Sum = 4+10+16=30.
    Total tilt = 0+0+0+2+7+6 = 15. Return `15`.

squirrel-simulation.goal: Calculate the minimum total distance a squirrel must travel to collect all nuts and bring them one by one to a tree. The squirrel starts at its initial position, picks up a nut, takes it to the tree, and then proceeds to the next nut.

squirrel-simulation.examples:

tree = [2,2]
squirrel = [4,4]
nuts = [[3,0], [2,5]]
Expected Output:
12
Explanation:
Distances:
Nut 1 ([3,0]): squirrel->nut = |4-3|+|4-0|=5, nut->tree = |3-2|+|0-2|=3. Total for nut 1 = 8.
Nut 2 ([2,5]): squirrel->nut = |4-2|+|4-5|=3, nut->tree = |2-2|+|5-2|=3. Total for nut 2 = 6.

Option 1: Pick nut 1 first, then nut 2.
Squirrel to nut 1: 5
Nut 1 to tree: 3
Tree to nut 2: |2-2|+|2-5|=3
Nut 2 to tree: 3
Total = 5 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 14

Option 2: Pick nut 2 first, then nut 1.
Squirrel to nut 2: 3
Nut 2 to tree: 3
Tree to nut 1: |2-3|+|2-0|=3
Nut 1 to tree: 3
Total = 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12
Minimum total distance is 12.

For Employee 1, the latest month is 4.

For Employee 2, the latest month is 3.
- For month 3 (which is >= 3): Cumulative salary = salary(month 2) + salary(month 1) = 30 + 20 = 50.
- For month 2 (which is < 3): Not included in output.

- Policyholder 4 (pid=4): `tiv_2015=10` (shared with pid 1, 3), `(lat,lon)=(40,40)` (unique). Both conditions met. Add 40 to sum.
Total sum = 10 + 40 = 50.00.

triangle-judgement.starterCode: SELECT CASE WHEN T1 + T2 > T3 AND T1 + T3 > T2 AND T2 + T3 > T1 THEN 'Yes' ELSE 'No' END AS triangle_type FROM Triangle;

design-excel-sum-formula.goal: Implement a basic spreadsheet functionality that allows setting cell values, retrieving cell values, and defining sum formulas for cells, ensuring formula results update automatically when dependencies change.
design-excel-sum-formula.constraints: 1 <= height, width <= 26 · Cells are indexed (row, col) or (char_col, row_num) · Cell values are integers · Formulas can refer to individual cells or ranges (e.g., A1, B2:C3).
design-excel-sum-formula.examples:
Excel(3, 3)
excel.set(1, 0, 5) // Cell (1,0) becomes 5
excel.set(1, 1, 6) // Cell (1,1) becomes 6
excel.sum(2, 1, ["A1", "A2"]) // Cell (2,1) becomes 11 (5+6)
excel.get(1, 0) // returns 5
excel.get(2, 1) // returns 11
excel.set(1, 0, 10) // Cell (1,0) updates to 10. Cell (2,1) should re-evaluate to 16.
excel.get(2, 1) // returns 16

smallest-range-covering-elements-from-k-.goal: Given 'k' sorted lists of integers, find the smallest range `[start, end]` (where `end - start` is minimized) that contains at least one number from each of the 'k' lists.
smallest-range-covering-elements-from-k-.constraints: 1 <= k <= 3500 · Each list `nums[i]` is sorted in non-decreasing order · 1 <= nums[i].length <= 50 · -10^5 <= nums[i][j] <= 10^5.
smallest-range-covering-elements-from-k-.examples:
nums = [[4,10,15,24,26], [0,9,12,20], [5,18,22,30]]
The range [20, 24] is the smallest. It includes 24 from list 0, 20 from list 1, and 22 from list 2.
Result: [20, 24]

find-the-derangement-of-an-array.goal: Calculate the number of derangements for an array of `n` distinct elements. A derangement is a permutation where no element appears in its original position. Return the result modulo `10^9 + 7`.
find-the-derangement-of-an-array.examples:
n = 1 -> 0
n = 2 -> 1 (e.g., for [1,2], only [2,1] is a derangement)
n = 3 -> 2 (e.g., for [1,2,3], [2,3,1] and [3,1,2] are derangements)

design-log-storage-system.goal: Design a system to store log entries, each with a unique ID and a timestamp, and efficiently retrieve log IDs within a specified time range and granularity.
design-log-storage-system.constraints: 1 <= timestamp.length <= 14 · timestamp format: 'YYYY:MM:DD:HH:MM:SS' · granularity can be 'Year', 'Month', 'Day', 'Hour', 'Minute', 'Second'.

exclusive-time-of-functions.goal: Given the number of functions `n` and a list of logs representing function start/end events with timestamps, calculate the exclusive execution time for each function.
exclusive-time-of-functions.constraints: 1 <= n <= 100 · 1 <= logs.length <= 500 · log format: 'function_id:start/end:timestamp' · Timestamps are non-negative integers.
exclusive-time-of-functions.examples:
n = 2, logs = ["0:start:0", "1:start:2", "1:end:5", "0:end:6"]
- Function 0 starts at t=0.
- Function 1 starts at t=2, pausing Function 0.
- Function 1 ends at t=5. Its exclusive time is 5 - 2 + 1 = 4 units.
- Function 0 resumes at t=6 (after Function 1 ends at t=5).
- Function 0 ends at t=6. Its exclusive time is (2-0) + (6-5) = 2 + 1 = 3 units.
Result: [3, 4]

average-of-levels-in-binary-tree.goal: For a given binary tree, compute the average value of the nodes at each level and return these averages in an array, ordered from root level downwards.
average-of-levels-in-binary-tree.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4] · Node values are in the range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1].

shopping-offers.goal: Given individual item prices, a set of special offers (bundles of items at a fixed price), and the desired quantities of each item, find the minimum cost to purchase exactly the required items.
shopping-offers.constraints: 1 <= n <= 6 (number of items) · Prices and quantities for items are in [0, 100] · Number of special offers is in [0, 100] · Total items in an offer are in [0, 100].
shopping-offers.examples:
price = [2,5], special = [[3,0,5],[1,2,10]], needs = [3,2]
Item 0 costs 2, Item 1 costs 5.
Offer 0: 3 of Item 0 for 5.
Offer 1: 1 of Item 0, 2 of Item 1 for 10.
Needs: 3 of Item 0, 2 of Item 1.
Option 1: Buy all individually: 3*2 + 2*5 = 16.
Option 2: Use Offer 1 (1 of Item 0, 2 of Item 1 for 10). Remaining needs: 2 of Item 0. Buy individually: 2*2 = 4. Total: 10 + 4 = 14.
Option 3: Use Offer 0 (3 of Item 0 for 5). Remaining needs: 2 of Item 1. Buy individually: 2*5 = 10. Total: 5 + 10 = 15.
Minimum cost is 14.
Result: 14

Simplify RHS: x + 4

Solving for x: 2x - x = 4 - 2 => x = 2

design-circular-deque.goal: Design a double-ended queue (deque) with a fixed maximum capacity that operates in a circular manner, supporting insertion and deletion from both ends, and checking its state.
design-circular-deque.constraints: 1 <= k <= 1000 (maximum size of the deque) · All operations should be O(1) time complexity.
design-circular-deque.examples:
MyCircularDeque(3)
myCircularDeque.insertLast(1) // True
myCircularDeque.insertFront(2) // True
myCircularDeque.insertLast(3) // True
myCircularDeque.insertFront(4) // False (deque is full)
myCircularDeque.getRear() // returns 3
myCircularDeque.isFull() // returns True
myCircularDeque.deleteLast() // True
myCircularDeque.insertFront(4) // True
myCircularDeque.getFront() // returns 4

design-search-autocomplete-system.goal: Design an autocomplete system that suggests up to 3 most frequently typed sentences matching a given prefix. When a sentence is fully typed (ending with '#'), its frequency should be updated.
design-search-autocomplete-system.constraints: 1 <= sentences.length <= 100 · 1 <= sentence.length <= 100 · 1 <= times[i] <= 1000 · Input character is a lowercase English letter, a space, or '#'.
design-search-autocomplete-system.examples:
sentences = ["i love you", "island", "iroman", "i love leetcode"]
times = [5, 3, 2, 2]
AutocompleteSystem(sentences, times)
autocompleteSystem.input('i') -> ["i love you", "island", "i love leetcode"]
autocompleteSystem.input(' ') -> ["i love you", "i love leetcode"]
autocompleteSystem.input('a') -> []
autocompleteSystem.input('#') -> [] (sentence "i a" is now recorded with frequency 1)
autocompleteSystem.input('i') -> ["i love you", "island", "i love leetcode"]

set-mismatch.goal: You are given an array `nums` that originally contained all integers from `1` to `n` exactly once. Due to an error, one number was duplicated, and another was lost. Find the number that occurs twice and the number that is missing.

Number 2 is duplicated. Number 3 is missing.
Result: [2, 3]

maximum-length-of-pair-chain.goal: Given a list of pairs `(a, b)` where `a < b`, find the longest possible chain of pairs such that for any pair `(c, d)` immediately following `(a, b)` in the chain, `b < c`.

A valid chain: [1,2] -> [3,4]. Length is 2.
[2,3] cannot follow [1,2] because 2 is not strictly less than 2.
Result: 2

falling-squares.goal: Given a sequence of squares falling onto an infinite number line, return a list representing the maximum height achieved by any square after each square has fallen. Squares land on the highest point below them, stacking if they overlap.

2. [2,3] falls, lands on [1,2] at height 2. Top at 2+3=5. Max height: 5.
3. [6,1] falls, lands at height 0. Top at 0+1=1. Max height: 5.

flipping-an-image.goal: Given a binary matrix, first reverse each row horizontally, then invert all the pixel values (0s become 1s, and 1s become 0s). Return the resulting modified image.
flipping-an-image.constraints: n x n matrix · 1 <= n <= 20 · image[i][j] is 0 or 1.

find-and-replace-in-string.goal: Given a string `s`, along with lists of `indices`, `sources`, and `targets`, perform all specified replacement operations simultaneously. An operation replaces a substring `s[indices[i]...indices[i] + len(sources[i]) - 1]` with `targets[i]`, but only if the substring at `indices[i]` exactly matches `sources[i]`. All replacement operations are guaranteed not to overlap.
find-and-replace-in-string.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 1000 · 0 <= indices.length <= 100 · 1 <= sources[i].length, targets[i].length <= 100 · indices[i] are unique and sorted.
find-and-replace-in-string.examples:
s = "abcd", indices = [0, 2], sources = ["a", "cd"], targets = ["eee", "ffff"] -> "eeebffff"
At index 0, "a" matches "a", replace with "eee".
At index 2, "cd" matches "cd", replace with "ffff".
Result: "eeebffff".

sum-of-distances-in-tree.goal: For every node in a given tree, compute the sum of distances from that node to all other nodes in the tree. Return an array where `ans[i]` stores this sum for node `i`.
sum-of-distances-in-tree.constraints: 1 <= n <= 10^4 · The input `edges` form a valid tree.
sum-of-distances-in-tree.examples:
n = 2, edges = [[0,1]] -> [1,1]
For node 0: dist(0,0)=0, dist(0,1)=1. Sum = 1.
For node 1: dist(1,1)=0, dist(1,0)=1. Sum = 1.

image-overlap.goal: Given two square binary matrices, `img1` and `img2`, find the maximum number of overlapping `1`s that can be achieved by translating `img2` over `img1`. `img2` can be shifted horizontally and/or vertically relative to `img1`.
image-overlap.constraints: n x n matrices · 1 <= n <= 30 · img[i][j] is 0 or 1.
image-overlap.examples:
img1 = [[1,1,0],[0,1,0],[0,1,0]], img2 = [[0,0,0],[0,1,1],[0,0,1]] -> 3
If img2 is shifted such that its (0,0) aligns with img1's (1,1) (i.e., img2 is shifted down 1 and right 1 relative to img1):
img1_ones = [(0,0), (0,1), (1,1), (2,1)]
img2_ones = [(1,1), (1,2), (2,2)]
Relative displacement (r1-r2, c1-c2) of img1's 1s to img2's 1s:
(0,0)-(1,1) -> (-1,-1)
(0,1)-(1,2) -> (-1,-1)
(1,1)-(2,2) -> (-1,-1)
This displacement (-1,-1) occurs 3 times, which is the maximum overlap.

rectangle-overlap.goal: Determine if two given axis-aligned rectangles, `rec1` and `rec2`, overlap. They are considered to overlap if their intersection forms an area strictly greater than zero.
rectangle-overlap.constraints: All coordinates are integers · rect[i] is [x1, y1, x2, y2] where (x1, y1) is bottom-left and (x2, y2) is top-right · x1 < x2 and y1 < y2.
rectangle-overlap.examples:
rec1 = [0,0,2,2], rec2 = [1,1,3,3] -> True
The rectangles intersect in the region [1,1,2,2], which has a positive area.

new-21-game.goal: In a game, you start with 0 points. Each turn, you draw a card with an integer value from 1 to `maxPts`. You stop drawing cards once your total points reach or exceed `k`. Calculate the probability that your final score will be less than or equal to `n`.
new-21-game.examples:
n = 10, k = 1, maxPts = 10 -> 1.00000
You stop drawing when points >= 1. You draw a card (1 to 10). Your score becomes 1 to 10. All these scores are <= 10 (n). So the probability is 1.0.

push-dominoes.goal: Given a string representing a row of dominoes ('.', 'L', or 'R'), simulate how they fall. An 'L' causes dominoes to its left to fall left, and an 'R' causes dominoes to its right to fall right. A '.' domino between an 'L' and an 'R' falls towards the closer one; if equidistant, it remains standing. Return the final state of the dominoes.
push-dominoes.examples:
dominoes = ".L.R...LR..L.." -> "LL.R.LLRRLL..L"
Initial: .L.R...LR..L..
After first pass (L to left, R to right): LL.R.RR LR.LL.L
Final state after all interactions: LL.R.LLRRLL..L

similar-string-groups.goal: Given a list of strings, group them such that two strings are in the same group if they are "similar". Two strings are similar if one can be transformed into the other by swapping exactly two characters, or if they are identical. Similarity is transitive. Return the total number of distinct groups.
similar-string-groups.constraints: 1 <= strs.length <= 100 · 1 <= strs[i].length <= 1000 · All strings have the same length · All strings consist of lowercase English letters · strs[i] are unique.
similar-string-groups.examples:
strs = ["tars","rats","arts","star"] -> 1
"tars" and "rats" are similar (swap 't' and 'r').
"rats" and "arts" are similar (swap 'r' and 'a').
"arts" and "star" are similar (swap 'a' and 's').
Since similarity is transitive, all four strings belong to a single group.

magic-squares-in-grid.goal: Given a grid of integers, count the number of `3x3` subgrids that are "magic squares". A `3x3` magic square must contain all numbers from 1 to 9 exactly once, and the sum of numbers in each row, each column, and both main diagonals must be equal.
magic-squares-in-grid.examples:
grid = [[1,2,3,4],[5,8,1,6],[7,3,5,7],[8,4,9,2]] -> 1
The 3x3 subgrid starting at grid[1][1] is:
8 1 6
3 5 7
4 9 2
This is a magic square (all sums equal 15, contains 1-9 exactly once).

keys-and-rooms.goal: You are given `n` rooms, numbered 0 to `n-1`. Each room `i` contains a list of keys `rooms[i]`, where each key is the number of another room. Starting in room 0, determine if it's possible to visit every single room.
keys-and-rooms.constraints: n is number of rooms · 1 <= n <= 1000 · 0 <= rooms[i][j] < n · All keys rooms[i][j] are unique.
keys-and-rooms.examples:
rooms = [[1],[2],[3],[]] -> True
Start in room 0, get key 1.
Go to room 1, get key 2.
Go to room 2, get key 3.
Go to room 3, get no keys.
All rooms (0, 1, 2, 3) visited.

split-array-into-fibonacci-sequence.goal: Given a string `num` representing a large non-negative integer, split it into a Fibonacci-like sequence. A valid sequence must have at least three elements, where each element `F[i] + F[i+1] = F[i+2]`. Each number in the sequence must fit within a 32-bit signed integer `[0, 2^31 - 1]`, and numbers cannot have leading zeros (unless the number itself is "0"). Return any valid Fibonacci-like sequence as a list of integers, or an empty list if none exists.
split-array-into-fibonacci-sequence.constraints: 1 <= num.length <= 200 · num consists only of digits.
split-array-into-fibonacci-sequence.examples:
num = "123456579" -> [123, 456, 579]
Here, 123 + 456 = 579, forming a valid Fibonacci-like sequence.

guess-the-word.goal: This is an interactive problem. Your task is to guess a secret 6-letter word from a given `wordlist` within 10 attempts. You interact with a `Master` API that provides a `guess(word)` method. This method returns the number of exact character matches (same character at the same position) between your guessed word and the secret word. If you guess the secret word, `guess` returns 6.
guess-the-word.constraints: secret.length == 6 · wordlist.length <= 100 · All words in wordlist are unique and have length 6 · secret is one of the words in wordlist.
guess-the-word.examples:
secret = "acckzz", wordlist = ["acckzz","ccbazz","eiowzz","abcczz"]
If you call master.guess("acckzz"), it returns 6, and you win.
If you call master.guess("ccbazz"), it returns 3 (c,z,z match).
If you call master.guess("eiowzz"), it returns 2 (z,z match).

backspace-string-compare.goal: Given two strings `s` and `t`, determine if they become identical after processing backspace characters. A '#' character acts as a backspace, deleting the character immediately preceding it. An empty string with a backspace remains empty.
backspace-string-compare.constraints: 1 <= s.length, t.length <= 200 · s and t contain lowercase English letters and '#'.
backspace-string-compare.examples:
s = "ab#c", t = "ad#c" -> True
String s becomes "ac" (a -> ab -> a (backspace) -> ac).
String t becomes "ac" (a -> ad -> a (backspace) -> ac).
Since both result in "ac", they are equal.

longest-mountain-in-array.goal: Given an array of integers, find the length of the longest "mountain" subarray. A mountain subarray must contain at least three elements, strictly increase from left to a peak, and then strictly decrease to the right. If no such mountain exists, return 0.
longest-mountain-in-array.examples:
arr = [2,1,4,7,3,2,5] -> 5
The longest mountain subarray is [1,4,7,3,2], which has a length of 5.

hand-of-straights.goal: Given a hand of cards (an array of integers) and an integer `groupSize`, determine if it's possible to arrange all cards into groups of `groupSize` consecutive cards. Each card must be used exactly once.
hand-of-straights.examples:
hand = [1,2,3,6,2,3,4,7,8], groupSize = 3 -> True
The cards can be grouped as: [1,2,3], [2,3,4], [6,7,8].

shortest-path-visiting-all-nodes.goal: You are given an undirected, connected graph as an adjacency list. Find the length of the shortest path that visits every node in the graph at least once. You can start and end at any node, revisit nodes and edges, and traverse edges in both directions.
shortest-path-visiting-all-nodes.constraints: n == graph.length · 1 <= n <= 12 · 0 <= graph[i].length < n · graph[i] does not contain i · If graph[i] contains j, then graph[j] contains i.
shortest-path-visiting-all-nodes.examples:
graph = [[1,2,3],[0],[0],[0]] -> 4
A possible shortest path that visits all nodes (0,1,2,3) is 2 -> 0 -> 1 -> 0 -> 3, which has a length of 4.

shifting-letters.goal: Given a string `s` and an array `shifts` of the same length, apply a series of cumulative shifts to `s`. For each `shifts[i]`, all characters from `s[0]` up to `s[i]` are shifted forward by `shifts[i]` positions. A shift means 'a' becomes 'b', 'b' becomes 'c', ..., 'z' becomes 'a'. Return the final string after all shifts are applied.
shifting-letters.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^5 · s consists of lowercase English letters · shifts.length == s.length · 0 <= shifts[i] <= 10^9.
shifting-letters.examples:
s = "abc", shifts = [3,5,9] -> "rpl"
Initial: "abc"
1. Shift s[0...0] by 3: "dbc"
2. Shift s[0...1] by 5: "igc" (d+5=i, b+5=g)
3. Shift s[0...2] by 9: "rpl" (i+9=r, g+9=p, c+9=l)
Final: "rpl"

maximize-distance-to-closest-person.goal: You are given an array `seats` representing a row of seats, where `1` denotes a person sitting and `0` an empty seat. There is at least one empty seat and at least one person. Find the maximum distance an empty seat can be from its closest occupied seat.
maximize-distance-to-closest-person.constraints: 2 <= seats.length <= 2 * 10^4 · seats[i] is 0 or 1 · At least one 0 and one 1.
maximize-distance-to-closest-person.examples:
seats = [1,0,0,0,1,0,1] -> 2
If a person sits at index 2, their distance to the closest person (at index 0 or 4) is 2.
If a person sits at index 5, their distance to the closest person (at index 4 or 6) is 1.
The maximum possible distance is 2.

rectangle-area-ii.goal: Given a list of axis-aligned rectangles, calculate the total area covered by their union. Since the total area can be very large, return the result modulo `10^9 + 7`.
rectangle-area-ii.constraints: 1 <= rectangles.length <= 200 · rectangles[i] is [x1, y1, x2, y2] · 0 <= x1 < x2 <= 10^9 · 0 <= y1 < y2 <= 10^9.
rectangle-area-ii.examples:

Rectangle 1: [0,0,2,2] (Area 4)
Rectangle 2: [1,0,2,3] (Area 2)
Rectangle 3: [1,0,3,1] (Area 2)
The combined unique area covered by these rectangles is 6.

loud-and-rich.goal: You are given `n` people, each with a `quiet` value. You also have `richer` pairs, where `richer[i] = [a, b]` means person `a` is richer than person `b`. This richness relation is transitive. For each person `i`, find the person `j` who is either richer than `i` or is `i` themselves, and `j` has the minimum quietness value among all such people. Return an array `answer` where `answer[i]` is `j`.
loud-and-rich.constraints: n == quiet.length · 1 <= n <= 500 · 0 <= quiet[i] < 10^5 · 0 <= richer.length <= n * (n - 1) / 2 · 0 <= a, b < n · a != b · All richer pairs are unique.
loud-and-rich.examples:
quiet = [3,2,5,4,6,1,7,0], richer = [[1,0],[2,1],[3,1],[3,7],[4,3],[5,3],[6,3]] -> [5,5,2,5,4,5,6,7]
For person 0:
People richer than or equal to 0: {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.
Their quietness values: {quiet[0]=3, quiet[1]=2, quiet[2]=5, quiet[3]=4, quiet[4]=6, quiet[5]=1, quiet[6]=7}.
The minimum quietness is 1, belonging to person 5. So, answer[0] = 5.
For person 1:
People richer than or equal to 1: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.
Their quietness values: {quiet[1]=2, quiet[2]=5, quiet[3]=4, quiet[4]=6, quiet[5]=1, quiet[6]=7}.
The minimum quietness is 1, belonging to person 5. So, answer[1] = 5.

        self.left = left

    # Your code here
    pass

# Helper to build tree from list representation for testing
def build_tree(nodes: list[int | None]) -> TreeNode | None:
    if not nodes:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(nodes[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(nodes):
        current = queue.pop(0)
        if nodes[i] is not None:
            current.left = TreeNode(nodes[i])
            queue.append(current.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(nodes) and nodes[i] is not None:
            current.right = TreeNode(nodes[i])
            queue.append(current.right)
        i += 1
    return root

# expected: https://leetcode.com/problems/design-tinyurl

construct-binary-tree-from-string.goal: Given a string representation of a binary tree, reconstruct and return the root node of that tree. The string format is `number(left_child)(right_child)`, where missing children's parentheses might be omitted.
construct-binary-tree-from-string.constraints: The input string is valid · 1 <= s.length <= 10^4 · Numbers are integers.
construct-binary-tree-from-string.examples:
Input: s = "4(2(3)(1))(6(5))"
Output: [4,2,6,3,1,5]
Explanation: This represents the tree:
      4
     / \
    2   6
   / \ /
  3  1 5

complex-number-multiplication.goal: Given two complex numbers provided as strings (e.g., "a+bi"), calculate their product and return the result as a string in the same "a+bi" format.
complex-number-multiplication.constraints: Input strings are valid complex numbers a+bi · a, b are integers in [-100, 100].
complex-number-multiplication.examples:
Input: num1 = "1+1i", num2 = "1+1i"
Output: "0+2i"
Explanation: (1 + i) * (1 + i) = 1*1 + 1*i + i*1 + i*i = 1 + i + i - 1 = 0 + 2i.

convert-bst-to-greater-tree.goal: Transform a Binary Search Tree (BST) into a Greater Tree, where each node's value is updated to be the sum of its original value plus the sum of all values in the original BST that are greater than or equal to its original value.
convert-bst-to-greater-tree.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4] · Node values are unique integers in [-10^4, 10^4].
convert-bst-to-greater-tree.examples:
Input: root = [4,1,6,0,2,5,7,null,null,null,3,null,null,null,8]
Output: [30,36,21,36,35,26,15,null,null,null,33,null,null,null,8]
Explanation:
Original BST:
      4
     / \
    1   6
   / \ / \
  0  2 5  7
      \    \
       3    8
Greater Tree:
      30 (4+6+5+7+8)
     /  \
    36  21 (6+5+7+8)
   /  \ /  \
  36  35 26 15
       \     \
        33    8
convert-bst-to-greater-tree.starterCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

class Solution:
    def convertBST(self, root: TreeNode | None) -> TreeNode | None:
        # Your code here
        pass

# Helper for testing (e.g., to convert tree to list for comparison)
def tree_to_list(root):
    if not root: return []
    q = [root]
    res = []
    while q:
        node = q.pop(0)
        if node:
            res.append(node.val)
            q.append(node.left)
            q.append(node.right)
        else:
            res.append(None)
    while res and res[-1] is None: res.pop()
    return res

def list_to_tree(lst):
    if not lst: return None
    root = TreeNode(lst[0])
    q = [root]
    i = 1
    while q and i < len(lst):
        node = q.pop(0)
        if lst[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(lst[i])
            q.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(lst) and lst[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(lst[i])
            q.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

input_tree_list = [4,1,6,0,2,5,7,None,None,None,3,None,None,None,8]
root = list_to_tree(input_tree_list)
result_root = Solution().convertBST(root)
print(tree_to_list(result_root))

minimum-time-difference.goal: Given a list of 24-hour clock time points (e.g., "09:00"), find the smallest time difference in minutes between any two time points in the list. The difference can span across midnight.
minimum-time-difference.constraints: 2 <= timePoints.length <= 2 * 10^4 · Each time point is in "HH:MM" format · 00 <= HH < 24, 00 <= MM < 60.
minimum-time-difference.examples:
Input: timePoints = ["23:59","00:00"]
Output: 1
Explanation: The difference between "23:59" and "00:00" is 1 minute (considering wrap-around).

single-element-in-a-sorted-array.goal: In a sorted array where every element appears exactly twice except for one element that appears only once, find and return that unique element.
single-element-in-a-sorted-array.constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 10^5 · nums.length is odd · 0 <= nums[i] <= 10^5 · Solution must run in O(log n) time and O(1) space.

reverse-string-ii.goal: Given a string `s` and an integer `k`, reverse the first `k` characters for every `2k` characters starting from the beginning of the string. Handle cases where fewer than `k` or `2k` characters remain at the end.
reverse-string-ii.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^4 · 1 <= k <= 10^4 · s consists of lowercase English letters.
reverse-string-ii.examples:
Input: s = "abcdefg", k = 2
Output: "bacdfeg"
Explanation:
For "abcdefg", k=2:
- First 2k=4 chars "abcd": reverse first k=2 chars "ab" to "ba". String becomes "bacd".
- Remaining "efg": reverse first k=2 chars "ef" to "fe". String becomes "feg".
Combined: "bacdfeg".

01-matrix.goal: Given a binary matrix (containing only 0s and 1s), compute for each cell the distance to its nearest 0. The distance between two adjacent cells is 1.
01-matrix.constraints: m, n are dimensions · 1 <= m, n <= 10^4 · 1 <= m * n <= 10^4 · At least one 0 exists.
01-matrix.examples:
Input: mat = [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]
Output: [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]
Explanation: The '1' at (1,1) is 1 unit away from its neighboring '0's.

diameter-of-binary-tree.goal: Calculate the length of the longest path between any two nodes in a given binary tree. This path may or may not pass through the root, and its length is defined by the number of edges.
diameter-of-binary-tree.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4] · Node values are in [-100, 100].
diameter-of-binary-tree.examples:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]
Output: 3
Explanation: The longest path is from node 4 to node 5 (or 5 to 4), passing through node 2 and node 1. This path has 3 edges.
Tree:
      1
     / \
    2   3
   / \
  4   5

output-contest-matches.goal: Given `n` (a power of 2) representing the number of teams in a tournament, simulate the matches round by round and return the final match result as a string. In each round, the 1st team plays the n-th team, 2nd plays (n-1)-th, and so on, with winners proceeding.
output-contest-matches.constraints: 1 <= n <= 2^12 · n is a power of 2.
output-contest-matches.examples:
Input: n = 4
Output: "((1,4),(2,3))"
Explanation:
Round 1: (1,4), (2,3)
Round 2: ((1,4),(2,3))

boundary-of-binary-tree.goal: Given the root of a binary tree, return its boundary traversal. The boundary traversal consists of the root, then the left boundary (excluding leaves), then all leaves (from left to right), and finally the right boundary (excluding leaves, in reverse order).
boundary-of-binary-tree.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4] · Node values are in [-1000, 1000].
boundary-of-binary-tree.examples:
Input: root = [1,null,2,3,4]
Output: [1,3,4,2]
Explanation:
Tree:
   1
    \
     2
    / \
   3   4
Root: 1
Left boundary: None (2 is a right child, 3 is a leaf)
Leaves: 3, 4
Right boundary (reverse): 2
Combined: [1, 3, 4, 2]

remove-boxes.goal: Given an array of colored boxes, you can remove a contiguous subsegment of boxes of the same color. If you remove `k` boxes, you get `k*k` points. The goal is to maximize the total points by strategically removing boxes.

number-of-provinces.goal: Given an adjacency matrix representing direct connections between cities, determine the total number of distinct provinces. A province is a group of cities that are directly or indirectly connected.
number-of-provinces.constraints: n is the number of cities · 1 <= n <= 200 · isConnected[i][j] is 1 or 0 · isConnected[i][i] == 1 · isConnected[i][j] == isConnected[j][i].
number-of-provinces.examples:
Input: isConnected = [[1,1,0],[1,1,0],[0,0,1]]
Output: 2
Explanation: City 0 and 1 are connected, forming one province. City 2 is separate, forming another province.

split-array-with-equal-sum.goal: Determine if an integer array `nums` can be split into four non-empty contiguous subsegments, each having an equal sum. The split points `i, j, k` must satisfy `0 < i < j < k < n-1`.
split-array-with-equal-sum.examples:
Input: nums = [1,2,1,2,1,2,1]
Output: True
Explanation:
If i = 1, j = 3, k = 5:
Sum(0,0) = nums[0] = 1
Sum(2,2) = nums[2] = 1
Sum(4,4) = nums[4] = 1
Sum(6,6) = nums[6] = 1
All four subsegment sums are 1.

binary-tree-longest-consecutive-sequence.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4] · Node values are in [-10^9, 10^9].
binary-tree-longest-consecutive-sequence.examples:
Input: root = [2,1,3]
Output: 3
Explanation:
Tree:
   2
  / \
 1   3
Longest consecutive path is 1-2-3 (increasing) or 3-2-1 (decreasing), with length 3.

game-play-analysis-iv.goal: Calculate the fraction of players who logged in on the day immediately following their very first login. Return the result rounded to two decimal places.
game-play-analysis-iv.constraints: Activity table has player_id, device_id, event_date, games_played · (player_id, event_date) is the primary key · event_date is DATE.
game-play-analysis-iv.examples:
Input:
Activity table:
player_id | device_id | event_date | games_played
----------|-----------|------------|-------------
1         | 2         | 2016-03-01 | 5
1         | 2         | 2016-03-02 | 6
2         | 3         | 2017-06-25 | 1
3         | 1         | 2016-03-02 | 10
3         | 3         | 2016-03-03 | 1
Output:
fraction
--------
0.67
Explanation:
Player 1: First login 2016-03-01. Next login 2016-03-02. This is consecutive.
Player 2: First login 2017-06-25. No next login. Not consecutive.
Player 3: First login 2016-03-02. Next login 2016-03-03. This is consecutive.
2 out of 3 players had consecutive logins. 2/3 = 0.666... = 0.67.
game-play-analysis-iv.starterCode:
# Write your MySQL query statement below

SELECT
    ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT T1.player_id) / (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT player_id) FROM Activity), 2) AS fraction
FROM
    Activity T1
JOIN
    (
        SELECT
            player_id,
            MIN(event_date) AS first_login
        FROM
            Activity
        GROUP BY
            player_id
    ) T2
ON
    T1.player_id = T2.player_id AND DATEDIFF(T1.event_date, T2.first_login) = 1;

second-minimum-node-in-a-binary-tree.goal: In a special binary tree where each node's value is the minimum of its children's values, find the second smallest value among all nodes. Return -1 if no such second minimum exists.
second-minimum-node-in-a-binary-tree.constraints: The tree is non-empty · Each node has either two children or no children · If a node has two children, its value is the minimum of its children's values · Node values are positive integers.
second-minimum-node-in-a-binary-tree.examples:
Input: root = [2,2,5,null,null,5,7]
Tree:
    2
   / \
  2   5
     / \
    5   7
Output: 5
Explanation: The smallest value is 2, and the second smallest is 5.

bulb-switcher-ii.goal: Given `n` light bulbs, all initially on, and `m` operations, determine the number of distinct final configurations of the bulbs. The four available operations are: flip all bulbs, flip bulbs with even indices, flip bulbs with odd indices, or flip bulbs with indices `3k+1`.
bulb-switcher-ii.examples:
Input: n = 1, m = 1
Output: 2
Explanation: Initial state: [on]. After one operation, possible states are [on] (if no operation is applied, or an operation that results in [on]) and [off]. Operations: flip all -> [off], flip even -> [on], flip odd -> [off], flip 3k+1 -> [off]. Distinct states are [on], [off].

Input: n = 2, m = 1
Output: 3
Explanation: Initial state: [on, on]. After one operation, possible states are:
Flip all: [off, off]
Flip even (bulb 2): [on, off]
Flip odd (bulb 1): [off, on]
Flip 3k+1 (bulb 1): [off, on]
Distinct states are [off, off], [on, off], [off, on].

number-of-longest-increasing-subsequence.goal: Given an array of integers, find the number of longest increasing subsequences (LIS).
number-of-longest-increasing-subsequence.examples:
Input: nums = [1,3,5,4,7]
Output: 2
Explanation: The two longest increasing subsequences are [1, 3, 5, 7] and [1, 3, 4, 7]. Both have length 4.

longest-continuous-increasing-subsequenc.goal: Find the length of the longest continuous increasing subsequence in a given array of integers.
longest-continuous-increasing-subsequenc.examples:
Input: nums = [1,3,5,4,7]
Output: 3
Explanation: The longest continuous increasing subsequence is [1,3,5] with length 3. Although [1,3,5,7] is an increasing subsequence, it's not continuous because 5 is followed by 4.

cut-off-trees-for-golf-event.goal: Given a 2D grid representing a forest, find the minimum steps to cut down all trees (values > 1) in increasing order of height. You start at (0,0), can only move to adjacent cells, and obstacles (0) cannot be traversed. Return -1 if it's impossible to cut all trees.
cut-off-trees-for-golf-event.constraints: m == forest.length · n == forest[i].length · 1 <= m, n <= 50 · 0 <= forest[i][j] <= 10^9.
cut-off-trees-for-golf-event.examples:
Input: forest = [[1,2,3],[0,0,4],[7,6,5]]
Output: 6
Explanation:
Start at (0,0).
1. Cut tree at (0,1) (height 2): path (0,0)->(0,1) takes 1 step.
2. Cut tree at (0,2) (height 3): path (0,1)->(0,2) takes 1 step.
3. Cut tree at (1,2) (height 4): path (0,2)->(1,2) takes 1 step.
4. Cut tree at (2,2) (height 5): path (1,2)->(2,2) takes 1 step.
5. Cut tree at (2,1) (height 6): path (2,2)->(2,1) takes 1 step.
6. Cut tree at (2,0) (height 7): path (2,1)->(2,0) takes 1 step.
Total steps: 1+1+1+1+1+1 = 6.

implement-magic-dictionary.goal: Design a `MagicDictionary` class that allows you to add words to a dictionary and then search for words that differ by exactly one character from a given search word.
implement-magic-dictionary.constraints: All words consist of lowercase English letters · 1 <= word.length <= 100 · 1 <= dictionary.length <= 100 · 1 <= searchWord.length <= 100 · buildDict will be called once · search will be called at most 10^4 times.
implement-magic-dictionary.examples:
Input:
["MagicDictionary", "buildDict", "search", "search", "search", "search"]
[[], [["hello", "leetcode"]], ["hello"], ["hhllo"], ["hell"], ["leetcoded"]]
Output:
[null, null, false, true, false, false]
Explanation:
MagicDictionary magicDict = new MagicDictionary();
magicDict.buildDict(["hello", "leetcode"]);
magicDict.search("hello"); // Returns false (exact match, not one char difference)
magicDict.search("hhllo"); // Returns true (differs by 'h' vs 'e' at index 1)
magicDict.search("hell"); // Returns false (length mismatch)
magicDict.search("leetcoded"); // Returns false (length mismatch)

map-sum-pairs.goal: Implement a `MapSum` class that supports inserting key-value pairs (where keys are strings and values are integers) and querying the sum of values for all keys that begin with a given prefix.
map-sum-pairs.constraints: All keys and prefixes consist of lowercase English letters · 1 <= key.length, prefix.length <= 50 · 1 <= val <= 1000 · At most 50 calls to insert and 1000 calls to sum.
map-sum-pairs.examples:
Input:
["MapSum", "insert", "sum", "insert", "sum"]
[[], ["apple", 3], ["ap"], ["app", 2], ["ap"]]
Output:
[null, null, 3, null, 5]
Explanation:
MapSum mapSum = new MapSum();
mapSum.insert("apple", 3);
mapSum.sum("ap");           // return 3 (only "apple" starts with "ap")
mapSum.insert("app", 2);
mapSum.sum("ap");           // return 3 + 2 = 5 ("apple" and "app" start with "ap")

valid-parenthesis-string.goal: Determine if a string containing '(', ')', and '*' characters is valid. A valid string follows these rules: every '(' must have a corresponding ')', every ')' must have a corresponding '(', '(' must appear before its matching ')', and '*' can act as a single '(', a single ')', or an empty string.
valid-parenthesis-string.examples:
Input: s = "(*)"
Output: true
Explanation: '*' can be treated as an empty string, making it "()".

Input: s = "(*))"
Output: true
Explanation: '*' can be treated as '(', making it "()()".

24-game.goal: Given four integers, determine if it's possible to use standard arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /) and parentheses to make their result exactly 24. Each number must be used exactly once.
24-game.examples:
Input: cards = [4,1,8,7]
Output: true
Explanation: (8 - 4) * (7 - 1) = 4 * 6 = 24

Input: cards = [1,2,1,2]
Output: false

valid-palindrome-ii.goal: Given a string, determine if it can be transformed into a palindrome by deleting at most one character.
valid-palindrome-ii.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^5 · s consists of lowercase English letters.
valid-palindrome-ii.examples:
Input: s = "aba"
Output: true

Input: s = "abca"
Output: true
Explanation: Delete 'c'.

Input: s = "abc"
Output: false

next-closest-time.goal: Given a time in "HH:MM" format, find the next closest time using only the digits present in the original time. The resulting time must be strictly later than the input time, potentially wrapping around to the next day.
next-closest-time.constraints: The input time is always valid · Digits are '0'-'9'.
next-closest-time.examples:
Input: time = "19:34"
Output: "19:39"
Explanation: The digits available are 1, 9, 3, 4. The next closest time is 19:39.

Input: time = "23:59"
Output: "22:22"
Explanation: The digits available are 2, 3, 5, 9. The next closest time is 22:22, which is on the next day.

baseball-game.goal: Calculate the total score of a baseball game based on a list of operations. Operations can be an integer (score), '+', 'D', or 'C'.
baseball-game.constraints: 1 <= operations.length <= 1000 · operations[i] is "C", "D", "+", or a string representing an integer in [-100, 100] · For "+", there will always be at least two previous valid scores · For "C" and "D", there will always be at least one previous valid score.
baseball-game.examples:
Input: operations = ["5","2","C","D","+"]
Output: 30
Explanation:
"5" - Add 5 to the record. Record: [5]
"2" - Add 2 to the record. Record: [5, 2]
"C" - Invalidate and remove the previous score (2). Record: [5]
"D" - Add 2 * 5 = 10 to the record. Record: [5, 10]
"+" - Add 5 + 10 = 15 to the record. Record: [5, 10, 15]
Total score: 5 + 10 + 15 = 30

k-empty-slots.goal: Given `n` flowers blooming on specific days, find the earliest day when there are two blooming flowers with exactly `k` empty (unbloomed) slots between them. Return -1 if no such day exists.
k-empty-slots.constraints: n == flowers.length · 1 <= n <= 2 * 10^4 · 1 <= flowers[i] <= n · All flowers[i] are unique · 0 <= k <= n-1.
k-empty-slots.examples:
Input: flowers = [1,3,2], k = 1
Output: 2
Explanation:
Day 1: Flower at position 1 blooms. State: [1,0,0]
Day 2: Flower at position 3 blooms. State: [1,0,1]
Now, flower at 1 and flower at 3 have exactly one empty slot (position 2) between them. So, day 2 is the answer.

redundant-connection.goal: Given a list of edges representing a graph that was originally a tree but had one extra edge added, find this redundant edge. If multiple such edges could form a cycle, return the one that appears last in the input list.
redundant-connection.constraints: n is the number of nodes · 3 <= n <= 1000 · edges.length == n · edges[i].length == 2 · 1 <= ui, vi <= n · ui != vi · There are no parallel edges or self-loops.
redundant-connection.examples:
Input: edges = [[1,2],[1,3],[2,3]]
Output: [2,3]
Explanation: Adding [2,3] creates a cycle 1-2-3-1.

Input: edges = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[1,4],[1,5]]
Output: [1,4]
Explanation: Adding [1,4] creates a cycle 1-2-3-4-1.

redundant-connection-ii.goal: Given a directed graph with `n` nodes and `n` edges, which was originally a rooted tree with one extra edge, find the redundant edge. The added edge either creates a cycle or causes a node to have two parents. If multiple answers exist, return the one that appears last in the input list.
redundant-connection-ii.constraints: n == edges.length · 3 <= n <= 1000 · edges[i].length == 2 · 1 <= ui, vi <= n.
redundant-connection-ii.examples:
Input: edges = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,1]]
Output: [3,1]
Explanation: Adding [3,1] creates a cycle 1->2->3->1.

Input: edges = [[1,2],[2,3],[1,3]]
Output: [1,3]
Explanation: Node 3 has two parents (2 and 1). [1,3] is the redundant edge.

repeated-string-match.goal: Given two strings `a` and `b`, determine the minimum number of times `a` must be repeated such that `b` becomes a substring of the repeated `a`. If `b` can never be a substring, return -1.
repeated-string-match.constraints: 1 <= a.length, b.length <= 10^4 · a and b consist of lowercase English letters.
repeated-string-match.examples:
Input: a = "abcd", b = "cdabcdab"
Output: 3
Explanation: Repeating "abcd" three times gives "abcdabcdabcd". "cdabcdab" is a substring of this.

Input: a = "a", b = "aa"
Output: 2

longest-univalue-path.goal: Find the length of the longest path in a binary tree where all nodes along the path have the same value. The path length is measured by the number of edges.
longest-univalue-path.constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4] · -1000 <= Node.val <= 1000 · The depth of the tree will not exceed 1000.
longest-univalue-path.examples:
Input: root = [5,4,5,1,1,5]
Tree:
      5
     / \
    4   5
   / \   \
  1   1   5
Output: 2
Explanation: The longest univalue path is 4->1->1 (length 2) or 5->5->5 (length 2).

Input: root = [1,4,5,4,4,5]
Tree:
      1
     / \
    4   5
   / \   \
  4   4   5
Output: 2
Explanation: The longest univalue path is 4->4->4 (length 2).

knight-probability-in-chessboard.goal: Given an `n x n` chessboard, a knight starts at `(row, column)` and makes exactly `k` moves. Calculate the probability that the knight remains on the board after `k` moves.
knight-probability-in-chessboard.examples:
Input: n = 3, k = 2, row = 0, column = 0
Output: 0.06250
Explanation: The knight starts at (0,0).
Possible moves for k=1: (1,2), (2,1). Both are on board.
From (1,2), on-board moves for k=2: (0,0), (2,0). (2 paths)
From (2,1), on-board moves for k=2: (0,0), (0,2). (2 paths)
Total paths staying on board: 2+2 = 4.
Total possible paths (8 moves each step): 8^2 = 64.
Probability: 4/64 = 0.0625.

Row 1 needs at least 1 trailing zero. It has 1.
Row 2 needs at least 0 trailing zeros. It has 2.

Trailing zeros count for each row: [0, 1, 2]
Target trailing zeros: [2, 1, 0]

To make row 0 valid (needs 2 trailing zeros), we can swap it with row 2.
Grid becomes [[1,0,0],[1,1,0],[0,0,1]] (swapped original row 0 and row 2). Swaps = 1.
New trailing zeros: [2, 1, 0]
Now, row 0 has 2 trailing zeros (valid).
Row 1 has 1 trailing zero (valid).
Row 2 has 0 trailing zeros (valid).
The grid is valid. Minimum swaps = 1.

guess-the-majority-in-a-hidden-array.goal: You are given a hidden array of 0s and 1s. Your task is to determine if there are more 0s, more 1s, or an equal number of both. You can only use a special ArrayReader interface to query sums of subsegments.
guess-the-majority-in-a-hidden-array.constraints: ArrayReader has compareSub(l, r, x, y) returning 1 if sum(arr[l..r]) > sum(arr[x..y]), -1 if sum(arr[l..r]) < sum(arr[x..y]), 0 if equal · length() returns array size · Array size is between 1 and 100.
guess-the-majority-in-a-hidden-array.examples:
ArrayReader represents [0,0,1,1]. length() returns 4.
reader.compareSub(0, 0, 1, 1) compares arr[0] with arr[1]. Returns 0.
reader.compareSub(0, 1, 2, 3) compares arr[0]+arr[1] with arr[2]+arr[3]. Returns -1 (0+0 < 1+1).
The array has an equal number of 0s and 1s, so the result is 0.

ArrayReader represents [0,0,0,1]. length() returns 4.
reader.compareSub(0, 0, 1, 1) returns 0.
reader.compareSub(0, 1, 2, 2) compares arr[0]+arr[1] with arr[2]. Returns 1 (0+0 > 0).
The array has more 0s, so the result is -1.
guess-the-majority-in-a-hidden-array.starterCode:
class ArrayReader:
    def __init__(self, arr):
        self.arr = arr
    def compareSub(self, l, r, x, y):
        sum_lr = sum(self.arr[l:r+1])
        sum_xy = sum(self.arr[x:y+1])
        if sum_lr > sum_xy: return 1
        if sum_lr < sum_xy: return -1
        return 0
    def length(self):
        return len(self.arr)

def guessMajority(reader: 'ArrayReader') -> int:
    # Your code here
    pass

# Example usage:
reader = ArrayReader([0,0,1,1])
print(guessMajority(reader)) # expected: 0
reader = ArrayReader([0,0,0,1])
print(guessMajority(reader)) # expected: -1
reader = ArrayReader([0,1,1,1])
print(guessMajority(reader)) # expected: 1

can-convert-string-in-k-moves.goal: Given two strings `s` and `t` of equal length, and an integer `k`, determine if `s` can be transformed into `t`. Each character `s[i]` can be shifted by `x` positions (`1 <= x <= 25`) to become `t[i]`. If `s[i]` and `t[i]` are the same, no shift is needed. If multiple characters require the same shift value `x`, they must be performed at distinct "times": the first at time `x`, the second at `x+26`, the third at `x+2*26`, and so on. All required shifts must be performable within `k` "moves" (i.e., the maximum time used for any shift must not exceed `k`).
can-convert-string-in-k-moves.constraints: 1 <= s.length, t.length <= 10^5 · s.length == t.length · 0 <= k <= 10^9 · s and t consist of lowercase English letters.
can-convert-string-in-k-moves.examples:
s = "abc", t = "bcd", k = 10
To change 'a' to 'b', needs 1 shift.
To change 'b' to 'c', needs 1 shift.
To change 'c' to 'd', needs 1 shift.
All three characters require a shift of 1.
The first '1' shift can happen at time 1.
The second '1' shift must happen at a different time, minimum 1 + 26 = 27.
The third '1' shift must happen at a different time, minimum 1 + 2*26 = 53.
The maximum time required is 53. Since k=10 < 53, it's False.

s = "aab", t = "bbb", k = 2
s[0] ('a') -> t[0] ('b'): shift 1.
s[1] ('a') -> t[1] ('b'): shift 1.
s[2] ('b') -> t[2] ('b'): shift 0 (no shift needed).
Two characters require a shift of 1.
The first '1' shift can happen at time 1.
The second '1' shift must happen at a different time, minimum 1 + 26 = 27.
The maximum time required is 27. Since k=2 < 27, it's False.

minimum-insertions-to-balance-a-parenthe.goal: Given a string `s` consisting only of `'('` and `')'`, find the minimum number of insertions needed to make it a "balanced" parentheses string. A string is balanced if every opening parenthesis `(` has exactly two consecutive closing parentheses `))` matching it, and the parentheses are properly nested.
minimum-insertions-to-balance-a-parenthe.examples:
s = "(()))"
1. Read '(': open_count = 1.
2. Read '(': open_count = 2.
3. Read ')': This is `s[2]`. Check `s[3]`. It's also ')'. So `s[2]s[3]` forms `))`. This matches one open '('. open_count becomes 1. (Index advances past `s[3]`)
4. Read ')': This is `s[4]`. There's no `s[5]`. This single ')' needs another ')' to form `))`. Insert one ')'. open_count becomes 0. Insertions = 1.
End of string. open_count is 0. Total insertions: 1.

s = "))())("
1. Read ')': This is `s[0]`. No open '('. It needs a preceding '(' and another ')' to form '))'. Insert '('. Insertions = 1. Insert ')'. Insertions = 2. (Index advances past `s[1]` if it was `)`) - In this specific case, `s[1]` is `)`. So it forms `))`. We need to insert a `(`. Insertions = 1. `open_count` remains 0.
2. Read ')': This is `s[2]`. No open '('. It needs a preceding '(' and another ')' to form '))'. Insert '('. Insertions = 2. No next `)`. Insert ')'. Insertions = 3.
3. Read '(': open_count = 1.
4. Read ')': This is `s[4]`. One open '('. No next `)`. Need to insert one ')'. Insertions = 4. open_count becomes 0.
End of string. open_count is 0. Total insertions: 4.

2. Cut [0,3] at 1: Cost = 3 (cutting stick [0,3]). Remaining sticks: [0,1], [1,3].
3. Cut [3,7] at 5: Cost = 4 (cutting stick [3,7]). Remaining sticks: [3,5], [5,7].
4. Cut [3,5] at 4: Cost = 2 (cutting stick [3,5]). Remaining sticks: [3,4], [4,5].
Total cost = 7 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 16.
This is the minimum cost.

the-most-similar-path-in-a-graph.goal: Given a graph of `n` cities and `roads`, and an array of city names, find a path of length `targetPath.length` starting from any city that minimizes the total "edit distance" to `targetPath`. The edit distance between two paths is the sum of edit distances between corresponding city names. Return the sequence of city IDs that forms this most similar path.
the-most-similar-path-in-a-graph.constraints: 2 <= n <= 100 · roads.length <= n * (n - 1) / 2 · 1 <= names.length <= 5 · names[i] consists of lowercase English letters · 1 <= targetPath.length <= 100.
the-most-similar-path-in-a-graph.examples:
n = 5, roads = [[0,2],[0,3],[1,2],[1,3],[1,4],[2,4],[3,4]]
names = ["ATL","PEK","LAX","DXB","HND"]
targetPath = ["ATL","DXB","HND","LAX"]

Edit distance between two strings is the number of differing characters.
Example path: 0 -> 3 -> 4 -> 2 (representing cities ATL -> DXB -> HND -> LAX)

Comparing path [names[0], names[3], names[4], names[2]] with targetPath ["ATL","DXB","HND","LAX"]:
- dist(names[0], "ATL") = dist("ATL", "ATL") = 0
- dist(names[3], "DXB") = dist("DXB", "DXB") = 0
- dist(names[4], "HND") = dist("HND", "HND") = 0
- dist(names[2], "LAX") = dist("LAX", "LAX") = 0
Total edit distance = 0. This is the minimum possible, so the path [0,3,4,2] is a valid answer.

minimum-operations-to-make-array-equal.goal: You are given an array `arr` of length `n`, where `arr[i] = (2 * i) + 1`. In one operation, you can choose two indices `x` and `y` and decrease `arr[x]` by 1 and increase `arr[y]` by 1. The goal is to make all elements of the array equal. Find the minimum number of operations required.
minimum-operations-to-make-array-equal.examples:
n = 3
arr = [1, 3, 5]
The target value for all elements is the average, which is (1+3+5)/3 = 3.
To make arr[0]=1 equal to 3, it needs 2 increases.
To make arr[2]=5 equal to 3, it needs 2 decreases.
Since each operation involves one increase and one decrease, the total operations needed is the sum of differences for elements less than the target. For arr[0]=1, (3-1) = 2 operations. Total operations = 2.

n = 6
arr = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11]
The target value is (1+3+5+7+9+11)/6 = 36/6 = 6.
Elements less than 6: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=3, arr[2]=5.
Differences: (6-1)=5, (6-3)=3, (6-5)=1.
Total operations = 5 + 3 + 1 = 9.
